clauses.name: Mutuality
clauses.requirement: The NDA must bind both parties symmetrically as disclosing and receiving parties.
clauses.preferred_position: Mutual NDA: both parties act as Disclosing Party and Receiving Party, with symmetric confidentiality obligations.
clauses.check_trigger: One-way or non-mutual language that binds only one side, fixes one party as the only Receiving Party, or otherwise fails to make both parties disclosure and receipt parties.
clauses.redline_template: Each party may disclose Confidential Information under this Agreement, and each party acts as both a Disclosing Party and a Receiving Party with respect to Confidential Information it discloses or receives.
semantic_signals: the parties may disclose confidential information to each other
each party acts as both disclosing party and receiving party
clauses.rationale: A one-way NDA creates asymmetric risk; Aspora's baseline is reciprocal confidentiality unless the matter is deliberately unilateral.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Look for language tying both parties to disclosing and receiving roles, not only isolated defined terms.

rules.acceptable_position: Both parties can disclose and receive Confidential Information, and confidentiality obligations operate symmetrically.

pass_conditions.description: The contract clearly makes each party both a Disclosing Party and a Receiving Party, or otherwise creates reciprocal confidentiality obligations.

fail_conditions.description: No operative language clearly makes confidentiality obligations mutual.
The contract fixes only one party as the Receiving Party or otherwise creates one-way confidentiality obligations.

review_triggers.description: Mutuality appears only in a title, recital, definition, or separated role language that does not clearly create reciprocal obligations.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the operative language that creates reciprocal roles, or cite the one-way language that fixes only one receiving party.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use the mutuality template to add or replace language so both parties can disclose and receive Confidential Information.

clauses.acceptable_language: Each party may disclose Confidential Information and each party acts as both a Disclosing Party and Receiving Party.

clauses.name: Confidential Information
clauses.requirement: Confidential Information must be broad and must not include extra exclusions beyond the standard carve-outs.
clauses.preferred_position: Broad definition covering all non-public business, financial, technical, customer, employee, supplier, pricing, market, product, proprietary, trade secret and source code information, including the existence and terms of the Agreement, whether marked or unmarked.
clauses.check_trigger: The definition is missing, too narrow, carves out core categories, or includes exclusions beyond the standard public-domain, prior-possession, lawful third-party source, and qualified independent-development carve-outs.
clauses.redline_template: "Confidential Information" means any and all non-public business, financial, technical, customer, employee, supplier, pricing, market, product, proprietary, trade secret and source code information disclosed by either party, including the existence and terms of this Agreement, whether disclosed orally, visually, electronically or in writing and whether or not marked confidential. Confidential Information does not include information that is public through no breach of this Agreement, already known without confidentiality obligation, received from a lawful third party without confidentiality obligation, or independently developed without use of or reference to Confidential Information.
clauses.standard_exclusions_template: Confidential Information does not include information that is public through no breach of this Agreement, already known without confidentiality obligation, received from a lawful third party without confidentiality obligation, or independently developed without use of or reference to Confidential Information.
semantic_signals: information whether oral written electronic or visual
clauses.rationale: The definition should catch the business material actually exchanged in an NDA workflow without adding carve-outs that weaken protection.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Anchor on the defined term, then inspect the same paragraph for category breadth and nearby exclusion language.

rules.acceptable_position: Confidential Information is defined broadly enough to cover non-public business, financial, technical, customer, employee, supplier, pricing, market, product, proprietary, trade secret, and source code information, with only standard carve-outs.

pass_conditions.description: The definition covers the material categories in the playbook and contains only accepted exclusions.

fail_conditions.description: The document does not define Confidential Information.
The definition omits important protected categories or includes extra exclusions such as residuals, reverse engineering, or unqualified independent development.

review_triggers.description: The definition or nearby exclusions are ambiguous enough that a human should confirm whether protection is materially narrowed.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the defined term paragraph and any nearby exclusion language that supports the verdict.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use the full definition template for missing or narrow definitions; use the standard exclusions template when only exclusions need correction.

clauses.acceptable_language: Confidential Information means any and all non-public business, financial, technical, customer, supplier, pricing, market, proprietary and trade secret information disclosed by either party.

clauses.name: Governing Law
clauses.requirement: Governing law must be India, Delaware, England and Wales, or DIFC.
clauses.preferred_position: The governing law is one of the approved jurisdictions, preferably England and Wales unless the matter context supports another approved option.
clauses.check_trigger: The governing law is missing, unclear, or names a jurisdiction outside India, Delaware, England and Wales, or DIFC.
semantic_signals: agreement shall be construed in accordance with
clauses.rationale: The preferred jurisdictions are the approved operating set for this workflow; anything else should be changed or reviewed.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Use the governing-law sentence as evidence, not the whole dispute-resolution section unless needed.

rules.acceptable_position: The governing law is one of the approved jurisdictions in the playbook, with England and Wales as the default preferred option unless matter context supports another approved jurisdiction.

pass_conditions.description: The governing-law clause names India, Delaware, England and Wales, or DIFC.

fail_conditions.description: The governing-law clause names a jurisdiction outside the approved playbook list.

review_triggers.description: The governing-law language is unclear, conditional, conflicting, heading-only, or mixed with non-approved jurisdiction language.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the governing-law sentence. If missing, explain that no governing-law sentence was found.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use one of the approved jurisdiction options. Default to England and Wales unless another approved option is selected.

clauses.acceptable_language: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of England and Wales, India, Delaware, or the DIFC.

clauses.name: Term and Survival
clauses.requirement: The NDA term and ordinary confidentiality survival must be fixed at up to five years.
clauses.preferred_position: Ordinary confidentiality obligations survive for a fixed period of up to five years. Narrow trade-secret, legal/regulatory, and data-protection obligations may survive for as long as the protected status or law requires.
clauses.check_trigger: Ordinary confidentiality is perpetual, indefinite, relationship-based, tied to information remaining confidential, longer than five years, or missing a clear fixed term or survival period.
clauses.redline_template: The confidentiality obligations survive for a fixed period of up to {max_term_years_label}, except that trade secrets, legally required confidentiality, and personal data protected by data-protection law survive for as long as the protected status or law requires.
clauses.rationale: Ordinary confidentiality should be time-limited; indefinite language is only acceptable for narrow trade-secret or legal exceptions.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Prefer paragraphs containing duration language plus a year count or indefinite survival wording.

rules.acceptable_position: Ordinary confidentiality obligations have a fixed survival period of up to five years, while narrow trade-secret, legal, regulatory, or data-protection carve-outs may survive longer.

pass_conditions.description: The term or ordinary confidentiality survival period is fixed and does not exceed five years.

fail_conditions.description: No clear term or ordinary confidentiality survival period is found.
Ordinary confidentiality survival is indefinite, perpetual, relationship-based, tied to information remaining confidential, or longer than five years.

review_triggers.description: The term language is ambiguous, cross-referenced, unresolved, or unclear about whether the long survival applies only to accepted carve-outs.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the term or survival paragraph, including the duration or indefinite wording. If missing, explain that no clear term or survival period was found.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use the survival template with the playbook maximum term and narrow longer-survival carve-outs.

indefinite_terms: for so long as the information remains confidential
clauses.acceptable_language: The confidentiality obligations survive for a fixed period of up to five years, except for trade secrets or legal obligations that require a longer period.

clauses.name: Non-Circumvention
clauses.requirement: The NDA must not include non-circumvention, introduced-party non-solicit, or substitute-purpose exclusivity obligations.
clauses.preferred_position: The NDA should not restrict direct dealings, introduced contacts, substitute transactions, exclusivity, or similar commercial conduct beyond confidentiality.
clauses.check_trigger: Any non-circumvention, introduced-party non-solicit, substitute-purpose, direct-dealing, bypassing, or exclusivity restriction appears in the NDA.
clauses.rationale: Non-circumvention and substitute-purpose restrictions turn the NDA into a broader commercial restraint.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Flag paragraphs restricting introduced parties, direct dealings, bypassing, substitute transactions, or exclusivity.

rules.acceptable_position: The NDA does not restrict direct dealings, introduced contacts, substitute transactions, exclusivity, or similar commercial conduct beyond confidentiality.

pass_conditions.description: No non-circumvention, introduced-party non-solicit, substitute-purpose, direct-dealing, bypassing, or exclusivity restriction appears.

fail_conditions.description: The document includes a non-circumvention, introduced-party non-solicit, substitute-purpose, direct-dealing, bypassing, or exclusivity restriction.

review_triggers.description: The language may restrict business conduct beyond confidentiality, but the scope or polarity is ambiguous.

evidence_requirements.guidance: For failures, cite the exact restriction. For passes, absence of matching prohibited language may be enough.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Remove the prohibited restraint rather than replacing it with another commercial restriction.

clauses.acceptable_language: No non-circumvention, introduced-party non-solicit, substitute-purpose exclusivity, or similar restriction should appear in the NDA.

clauses.name: Signatures
clauses.requirement: The execution block must show both parties, signatory titles, and a date.
clauses.preferred_position: Both parties have complete execution blocks showing legal entity name, authorised signatory, title or capacity, and date.
clauses.check_trigger: The execution block is missing, one party block is absent, or signatory name, title/capacity, or date markers are incomplete.
semantic_signals: signed for and on behalf of
clauses.rationale: A complete execution block reduces signing ambiguity and supports clean downstream storage.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Look for both parties' execution blocks with signatory, title or capacity, and date markers.

rules.acceptable_position: Both parties have complete execution blocks showing legal entity name, authorised signatory, title or capacity, and date.

pass_conditions.description: Execution blocks show both parties, signatory markers, titles or capacities, and dates.

fail_conditions.description: No usable execution block is found.
One or both execution blocks are present but missing party, signatory, title, or date markers.

review_triggers.description: Signature material is present but it is unclear whether both parties and required markers are complete.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the execution block when present. If missing, explain that no complete execution block was found.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use the execution-block template when signatures are missing or materially incomplete.

clauses.redline_template:
For [Party 1 legal name]
By:
Name:
Title:
Date:

For [Party 2 legal name]
By:
Name:
Title:
Date:
clauses.acceptable_language: Each party should have an execution block showing the legal entity name, authorised signatory, title, and date.
